citation: Hamstring muscle injuries in elite football: translating research into practice.
Valente HG, Oliveira RR, Baroni BM. How are hamstring strain injuries managed in elite men's football clubs? A survey with 62 Brazilian physical therapists. Physical Therapy in Sport. 2023;61:73-81.
abstract: This cross-sectional survey described the perceptions and practices of 62 physical therapists from 35 of 40 elite Brazilian men's football clubs (87.5% representativeness) regarding hamstring strain injury (HSI) management. Despite heterogeneity in assessment practices, all respondents used imaging exams, adopted injury classification scales, and evaluated pain, range of motion, muscle strength, and functional status. Rehabilitation programs were typically divided into 3–4 phases. All respondents applied electrophysical agents and stretching; 98.4% applied strengthening exercises (93.5% including eccentrics); 96.8% used manual therapy; 95.2% used exercises mimicking football's functional demands; and 93.5% used lumbopelvic stabilization exercises. Muscle strength was the most frequently reported return-to-play criterion (71% of respondents).
tldr: A survey of 62 Brazilian elite football physical therapists reveals that HSI rehabilitation universally incorporates imaging, classification systems, eccentric strengthening, manual therapy, electrophysical agents, and lumbopelvic stabilization, with muscle strength being the top return-to-play criterion.
methods: Cross-sectional study; online survey of 62 physical therapists from 35 of 40 elite Brazilian football clubs (Series A and B, 2021 season); 30-question validated questionnaire covering club structure, HSI assessment practices, and rehabilitation approaches; exploratory data analysis with descriptive statistics.
findings: All 62 respondents used imaging exams and injury classification systems. 98.4% applied strengthening exercises (93.5% included eccentrics). 96.8% used manual therapy; 95.2% used sport-specific functional exercises; 93.5% used lumbopelvic stabilization. Muscle strength was the top RTP criterion (71%). Most programs were 3–4 phases. 76% used time since injury as a progression criterion. Electrophysical agents used by 100% of respondents, most commonly analgesic currents (93%), photobiomodulation (89%), and cryotherapy (87%).
limitations: Only Brazilian physical therapists participated, limiting generalizability to other national football contexts; cultural and training system differences may affect findings; self-reported questionnaire data is prone to inconsistent responses; women's football clubs not included.
practicalImplications: Elite football physical therapy for HSI consistently emphasizes eccentric strengthening, lumbopelvic stabilization, and functional football-specific exercises. Muscle strength testing should be a core component of RTP criteria. Electrophysical agents and manual therapy are widely used adjuncts. Practitioners should standardize assessment tools (e.g., inclinometer for ROM, dynamometer for strength) given evidence supporting their prognostic value for time to RTP.
athleteDev: Developing football players with HSI should receive structured 3–4 phase rehabilitation incorporating eccentric hamstring strengthening, lumbopelvic stabilization, dynamic stretching, and progressive sport-specific running drills. Objective strength and functional criteria should guide phase progressions rather than time alone.
rtp: Muscle strength is the most reported RTP criterion among Brazilian elite football physical therapists, followed by sprint/high-speed running performance. RTP decisions should incorporate objective strength testing (ideally with dynamometry), functional performance, and absence of pain. Psychological readiness is assessed by the majority of practitioners but rarely with validated questionnaires.

citation: Monk SH, Legarreta AD, Kirby P, Brett BL, Yengo-Kahn AM, Bhatia A, Solomon GS, Zuckerman SL. Imaging findings after acute sport-related concussion in American football players: A systematic review. Journal of Clinical Neuroscience. 2019;61:28-35.
abstract: This PRISMA-guided systematic review summarized advanced brain imaging findings after acute sport-related concussion (SRC) in American football players, focusing on study methodology and clinical significance. Eleven case-control studies met inclusion criteria (football players, imaging within 6 months of SRC, sample size >5). Eight of the 11 studies conducted 809 brain function comparisons, of which 149 (18%) were statistically significant. Nine of 11 studies (82%) reported positive immediate findings (within 1 week post-injury), but these studies were more likely to exhibit recall bias (86% vs. 0%) and lack baseline advanced neuroimaging (78% vs. 50%) than negative studies. Only 3 of 9 studies evaluating subacute findings (33%) reported positive results. Studies with positive findings in either time period were more likely to have recall bias and lack baseline imaging. The review concluded that positive neuroimaging changes after SRC appear transient and return to baseline within months, though methodological limitations complicate interpretation.
tldr: Advanced brain imaging (fMRI, DTI, MRS, ASL-MRI) frequently shows immediate post-concussion changes in American football players, but these findings are largely transient, resolving within weeks to months, and are confounded by recall bias and lack of baseline imaging in most studies.
methods: Systematic review (PRISMA); PubMed database search (February 2017); inclusion criteria: American football players, brain imaging within 6 months of SRC, sample size >5; 11 studies met criteria; quality assessed using Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (NOS) and 4 a priori methodological criteria (control selection, Type I error control, recall bias, baseline imaging); imaging modalities: fMRI (n=5), DTI/DKI (n=2), MRS (n=2), ASL-MRI (n=2); total of 809 comparisons analyzed.
findings: Nine of 11 studies (82%) reported positive immediate imaging findings; only 3 of 9 studies (33%) reported persistent subacute positive findings. Studies with positive findings were more likely to have recall bias (86% immediate, 100% subacute) and lack baseline neuroimaging (78% immediate, 100% subacute). The median time to initial imaging was 72 hours. fMRI studies made 441 comparisons (15% significant); DTI studies made 302 (24% significant). Most positive changes resolved within 1–6 months post-injury.
limitations: Only 11 studies met inclusion criteria; all were case-control designs with small sample sizes (median N=12 per group); majority demonstrated recall bias and lacked baseline advanced neuroimaging; insufficient control for Type I error in several studies; inability to correct for confounders such as psychiatric history and substance use; imaging modalities and protocols were heterogeneous across studies.
practicalImplications: Advanced neuroimaging findings after SRC in football players are largely transient and should not be used as sole clinical decision-making tools without baseline imaging. Practitioners should be cautious about interpreting imaging 'abnormalities' without pre-season baseline comparisons. Future neuroimaging research should standardize protocols and include baseline imaging to improve clinical relevance.
athleteDev: Football programs at all levels should consider implementing pre-season advanced neuroimaging baselines as part of athlete health infrastructure, given the documented transient neuroimaging changes after SRC. Subconcussive impacts accumulate over a season and may alter imaging findings even without diagnosed concussion, warranting ongoing monitoring in developing players.
rtp: Current advanced neuroimaging findings after SRC in football players are largely transient and correlate inconsistently with symptom severity. RTP decisions should not rely on advanced neuroimaging alone; clinical symptom resolution, neurocognitive testing, and standardized concussion protocols remain the appropriate primary tools. Baseline neuroimaging, when available, provides the most meaningful reference for post-concussion imaging comparison.

citation: Ro J, Brushwood AE, Mokha M. Impact of ankle injury history and pre-National Football League Draft training on lower limb coordination during running: A pilot study. Cureus. 2025;17(10):e93887.
abstract: This pilot study examined the influence of a recent ankle sprain history (RASH, within 6 months) and a six-week NFL draft preparation training camp on lower limb intersegmental coordination in elite American football players. Twelve RASH players were matched to 12 non-RASH controls by position category and treadmill run speed. Running mechanics at 23 km/h were assessed pre- and post-training via a 10-camera motion capture system. Hip-ankle (HA) and knee-ankle (KA) coordination angles and variability were quantified using modified vector coding across four running gait subphases. No significant group × time interactions or main effects were found for coordination angles or variability in HA or KA (p > 0.05). However, medium effect sizes emerged for HA second half of stance (η²p=0.075) and HA coordination variability in the first half of swing (η²p=0.121), and KA first half of stance (η²p=0.093) and second half of swing (η²p=0.091). The RASH group shifted toward more ankle-dominant HA coupling and changed KA from anti-phase ankle to in-phase ankle in early stance post-training, while controls remained stable.
tldr: NFL draft-eligible football players with a recent ankle sprain history exhibit altered hip-ankle and knee-ankle coordination compared to uninjured peers, adopting compensatory proximal-dominant strategies; a six-week elite training camp produced medium-effect adaptations toward more ankle-dominant control, though changes did not reach statistical significance in this small pilot.
methods: Pilot study; N=24 elite American football players (12 RASH, 12 non-RASH controls); matched by position category (Big, Big-skill, Skill) and treadmill run speed; 10-camera 3D motion capture (Vicon, 120 Hz) with instrumented split-belt treadmill (Bertec, 1000 Hz); 23 km/h treadmill run, 5-second capture; modified vector coding for hip-ankle and knee-ankle coordination angles and variability across 4 gait subphases; 2×2 mixed model ANOVA (group × time); partial eta-squared for effect sizes.
findings: No significant group × time interactions (p > 0.05) for any HA or KA coordination or variability measure. Medium effect sizes for HA second half of stance (η²p=0.075): RASH shifted from in-phase hip-dominant to in-phase ankle-dominant after training. KA first half of stance (η²p=0.093): RASH shifted from anti-phase ankle to in-phase ankle. HA coordination variability first half of swing (η²p=0.121): non-RASH increased more than RASH post-training. Angle-angle plots showed RASH had limited ankle dorsiflexion pre-training that improved post-training, with greater hip extension at takeoff.
limitations: Small pilot sample (n=12 per group) underpowered for statistical significance; chronic ankle instability not assessed; no diagnostic imaging to confirm residual ankle abnormalities; same footwear not ensured at both sessions; no clinical measures (strength, ROM) to contextualize coordination findings; training program designed for NFL combine performance, not ankle rehabilitation; results may not generalize to non-elite populations.
practicalImplications: Even medically cleared, asymptomatic football athletes with a recent ankle sprain history exhibit altered lower limb coordination that persists into high-speed running. Elite training can partially restore ankle-dominant control patterns. Sports medicine and performance staff should use motion capture or biomechanical screening post-ankle sprain — beyond symptom resolution — to identify residual coordination deficits before returning athletes to full speed work.
athleteDev: NFL draft preparation training that incorporates multi-planar lower extremity loading (plyometrics, resisted sprints, assisted sprints) can partially restore ankle-dominant coordination patterns in players with a recent ankle sprain history. Developing football players should receive ankle-specific neuromuscular retraining targeting dorsiflexion and intersegmental coupling, not just structural rehabilitation, before progressing to full speed running.
rtp: Ankle sprain RTP clearance based on symptom resolution alone may be insufficient for elite football players; residual coordination deficits in hip-ankle and knee-ankle coupling can persist and be detected with vector coding analysis at high running speeds. Biomechanical screening at 23 km/h (or sport-specific speeds) is recommended before RTP to ensure movement pattern normalization and reduce re-injury risk.

citation: O'Malley GR, Pema P, Sweeney P, Sarwar S, Cassimatis N, Knightly JJ, Goldstein I, Patel NV. Impact of cervical and lumbar spine surgeries on National Football League (NFL) player performance and return-to-play outcomes. Cureus. 2025;17(6):e85706.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study evaluated the impact of cervical and lumbar spine surgeries on NFL players' return-to-play (RTP) rates and performance using injury reports from 2005 to 2022. A total of 144 spine surgeries (77 lumbar, 67 cervical) were identified across 136 players. Players with lumbar surgery had a 61% RTP rate and an average performance rating increase of 6.3% (PFF grade: 68 pre → 71 post). Players with cervical surgery had a 47% RTP rate and an average performance rating decrease of 5.8% (PFF grade: 68 pre → 66.7 post). Lumbar surgeries were predominantly discectomies in linemen with higher BMIs; cervical surgeries were predominantly fusions in skill positions. Players with prior lumbar surgery had a significantly higher RTP rate (83% vs. 56%, p=0.020). Only 22% of cervical surgery patients showed performance improvement vs. 58% of lumbar patients (p=0.019).
tldr: NFL players undergoing lumbar spine surgery have higher return-to-play rates (61%) and performance improvements post-op compared to those undergoing cervical surgery (47% RTP, average performance decline), with position-specific patterns — linemen predominantly lumbar, skill positions predominantly cervical — highlighting the need for tailored RTP and rehabilitation protocols.
methods: Retrospective cohort study; NFL injury reports 2005–2022; N=136 players, 144 surgeries (77 lumbar, 67 cervical); data from NFL.com, ESPN, Fox Sports, CBS Sports, Pro-Football-Reference; performance via Pro Football Focus (PFF) grades; statistical analysis: independent t-tests, chi-squared, two-way ANOVA, linear and logistic regression (Python 3.10.12); RTP defined as appearance in at least one regular-season NFL game post-surgery.
findings: 77 lumbar surgeries: 61% RTP, average PFF +6.3% (68→71); 67 cervical surgeries: 47% RTP, average PFF -5.8% (68→66.7). Prior lumbar surgery history significantly predicted higher RTP (83% vs. 56%, p=0.020). Cervical surgery patients significantly less likely to improve performance post-op (22% vs. 58%, p=0.019). Lumbar surgeries predominantly discectomies (75%) in linemen (BMI 32.3); cervical surgeries predominantly fusions (39%) in skill positions (BMI 30.5). Above-average performers had greater performance decline post-surgery regardless of type.
limitations: PFF data unavailable before 2006 and for all players; many procedure types unspecified due to NFL privacy reporting requirements; study relies on public injury reports which may be incomplete; no standardized patient-reported outcomes available; specific surgical technique details often unavailable; retrospective design limits causal inference.
practicalImplications: NFL medical and performance staff should counsel players differently based on surgery type: lumbar surgery (especially discectomy) carries a substantially better RTP and performance prognosis than cervical surgery (especially fusion). Cervical fusion patients should be prepared for likely performance decline and reduced career longevity. Position-specific rehabilitation protocols are warranted.
athleteDev: Developing football players who undergo spine surgery face significant career implications. Lumbar disc herniations treated with discectomy carry a favorable prognosis. Programs should implement load management strategies for high-BMI linemen to reduce chronic lumbar stress. Cervical spine injuries in skill positions warrant early intervention and careful surgical planning to maximize RTP potential.
rtp: Lumbar surgery (particularly discectomy) yields a 61% RTP rate with performance improvement, while cervical surgery yields only 47% RTP with average performance decline. Players with prior lumbar surgery history have higher RTP rates (83%). RTP criteria post-spine surgery should incorporate position-specific functional testing and PFF-grade benchmarks in addition to clinical clearance, with cervical surgery patients requiring more conservative progressions than lumbar surgery patients.
